us-politics.headline: US Jets Downed Over Iran as War Escalates
us-politics.body:
Two US warplanes were shot down during operations in Iran, with at least one crew member still missing. Recovery helicopters were also hit during search efforts, raising the human cost of the conflict sharply. The Pentagon has not confirmed all details publicly.

The losses are drawing bipartisan unease on Capitol Hill. Republican Senator John Curtis called for ending offensive operations without a formal war declaration, while Rep. Nancy Mace voiced concern about escalation. Democrats slammed prediction market Polymarket for taking bets on the missing pilot's fate.

NPR notes Trump's wartime rhetoric departs from past presidential norms, with analysts warning the framing could complicate diplomatic off-ramps. The administration has not outlined a clear endgame.

sources.title: What to know about US fighter jets hit in Iran war
2 US helicopters hit during recovery efforts in Iran
GOP senator calls to end Iran operations without formal declaration of war
Two U.S. warplanes shot down; search ongoing in Iran for 1 missing crew member
Mace ‘concerned about escalation’ in Iran war
Democrat rips Polymarket for taking bets on fate of US pilot shot down over Iran
How Trump's wartime rhetoric differs from past presidents and why that matters

us-politics.headline: Cabinet Shake-Up Looms as Blanche Takes DOJ Helm
us-politics.body:
Todd Blanche has stepped into the role of acting Attorney General following Pam Bondi's departure, inheriting a department under intense political scrutiny. Blanche told reporters there is no evidence in the Epstein files that Epstein worked as a spy. Rep. Mace is still pushing for Bondi to testify on the matter.

Trump is weighing further Cabinet changes but wants to avoid what aides describe as a massive shake-up, according to the Washington Post. Senator Thom Tillis separately set a Jan. 6 red line for any incoming attorney general nominee, signalling that Senate confirmation could be contested.

FBI Director Kash Patel and Army Secretary Pete McCormick also warned publicly that foreign terror threats inside the US grew during the Biden years, a claim Democrats dispute.

sources.title: Trump weighs more Cabinet changes, but wants to avoid ‘massive shake-up’
Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
Blanche: No evidence in Epstein files he was a spy
Tillis sets Jan. 6 red line on attorney general nominee
Mace ‘not backing away’ from Bondi testifying on Epstein
Patel, McCormick warn foreign terror threats inside US grew during Biden years

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Draws Fire From Collins, Democratic States Sue on Voting
us-politics.body:
Trump's budget proposal includes a $10 billion fund for Washington DC construction and beautification alongside a defence spending plan, but Republican Senator Susan Collins broke with the White House, criticising deep cuts in the proposal. The defence budget outline has drawn mixed reactions across both parties.

Separately, Oregon and a coalition of Democratic-led states filed lawsuits challenging Trump's executive order restricting vote-by-mail, calling it a source of confusion, chaos and distrust. Oregon's attorney general argues the order conflicts with state law and the Constitution. Senate Democrats are also pressing the Treasury Department over reports that Trump's name may be added to US currency.

sources.title: Susan Collins knocks funding cuts in White House budget proposal
What to know about Trump’s defense budget proposal
Senate Democrats press Treasury Department on decision to add Trump name to currency
Trump proposes $10B fund for D.C. construction, beautification projects
Democratic-led states sue over Trump mail-in ballot order
‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail

us-politics.headline: Trump Signs College Sports Order; Good Friday Message Stirs Debate
us-politics.body:
Trump signed an executive order targeting reforms in college sports, his second such action on the issue. The order takes aim at athlete compensation rules and NCAA governance, though critics question whether executive power is the right tool for what has historically been a legislative or judicial matter.

On Good Friday, Trump issued a message declaring America needs God and touting what he called a resurgence of religion. Supporters framed it as a welcome affirmation of faith in public life. Critics argued it blurs the line between church and state. The message drew wide attention heading into the Easter weekend.

sources.title: Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports

au-politics.headline: Two arrested in Dezi Freeman investigation as case develops
au-politics.body:
Victoria Police have arrested two people as part of their investigation into the movements of Dezi Freeman, whose disappearance has drawn sustained public attention. The arrests mark a significant operational step, though police have not yet detailed the charges or the suspects' connection to Freeman.

The case centres on the Porepunkah area in Victoria's alpine northeast. Investigators have been working to piece together Freeman's last known movements, and the arrests suggest that work is producing results. Further details are expected as the investigation continues.

sources.title: Victoria police arrest two people as part of Dezi Freeman investigation
Two arrested as part of investigation into movements of Dezi Freeman

au-politics.headline: Fuel supply fears persist despite minister's reassurances
au-politics.body:
Energy Minister Chris Bowen says fewer service stations are running dry, pointing to strong national fuel reserves. But panic buying driven by supply anxiety has kept demand elevated, complicating the picture on the ground.

Farmers used the moment to push a separate grievance - urging supermarkets to pay fairer prices for fresh produce, arguing that tight margins leave agricultural businesses exposed when input costs like fuel spike. The dual pressure on food and fuel supply chains highlights broader vulnerabilities in Australia's logistics network that neither reassuring ministerial statements nor market signals have yet resolved.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Fuel demand stays high, as farmers urge supermarkets to pay more for fresh produce

au-politics.headline: Nine charged over alleged 'mother ship' drug smuggling operation
au-politics.body:
Nine people face charges over an alleged conspiracy to import large quantities of cocaine and methamphetamine into Australia using a vessel operating as a so-called mother ship in Australian waters. The operation unravelled after a trawler sank, alerting police to the alleged smuggling network.

Victoria Police coordinated the investigation, which targeted what authorities describe as a sophisticated syndicate. The scale of the alleged importation - measured in tonnes - places it among the larger drug interception cases in recent years. Charges have been laid but the matter is yet to be tested in court.

sources.title: Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Sunken trawler alerts police to alleged drug-smuggling plan

au-politics.headline: Sydney councils warn data centres threaten housing and health
au-politics.body:
Local councils in Sydney are raising alarms about a wave of proposed data centres, arguing the facilities risk causing localised blackouts, consuming land earmarked for housing, and generating noise and heat that affects nearby residents. The concerns put councils on a collision course with state and federal governments keen to attract digital infrastructure investment.

Critics of the councils' position say data centres are essential to Australia's economic and technological future and that opposition risks pushing investment offshore. The dispute reflects a wider tension between rapid infrastructure expansion and the planning systems designed to manage its impacts on communities.

sources.title: Sydney councils fear new datacentres could cause blackouts, block housing and affect locals’ health

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Escalates Into Regional Conflagration
world.body:
Now 36 days in, the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran has widened sharply. Tehran claims it downed two American warplanes, while Iranian missiles struck residential areas in central Israel. Israel simultaneously bombed bridges in Lebanon, drawing Lebanese civilians into a conflict already straining the region's infrastructure and population.

The Strait of Hormuz remains a flashpoint, with questions mounting over whether force will be used to reopen the critical oil shipping lane. The WHO reports more than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1. A US crew member is missing in Iran, with rescue teams describing the search as harrowing. A separate attack near a UAE gas facility killed at least one person, signalling the war's reach beyond its core theatres.

sources.title: Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?
Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran war live: Tehran downs 2 US warplanes; Israel bombs Lebanon bridges
Will force be used to reopen Strait of Hormuz?
Survivors of Iran bridge attack recall terror of US-Israeli strike
More than 20 attacks on Iranian healthcare facilities since March 1: WHO
At least one killed at UAE’s Habshan gas facility after intercepted attack

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Sends Back Stunning Images of Earth
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II mission has released its first photographs of Earth taken from deep space, offering images that astronauts described as spectacular. The pictures, captured as the crew travels toward the Moon, mark the first time humans have photographed Earth from that distance since the Apollo era.

The mission represents a major milestone in NASA's broader push to return humans to lunar orbit. The images have drawn widespread attention, underscoring both the scientific ambition of the programme and its value as a moment of collective perspective.

sources.title: NASA unveils first images of Earth from Artemis II
Artemis II crew take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA releases stunning first photos of Earth from Artemis II moon mission

world.headline: Cuba Frees 2,000 Prisoners as US Pressure Bites
world.body:
Cuba has begun releasing more than 2,000 prisoners in what analysts see as a direct response to sustained US pressure. The scale of the release is significant - it follows months of diplomatic and economic squeezing by Washington and represents one of the largest prisoner releases the island has seen in years.

The move raises questions about what, if anything, Havana expects in return. The US has not publicly signalled any easing of sanctions.

sources.title: Cuba begins releasing more than 2,000 prisoners as US pressure mounts
Cuba releases over 2,000 prisoners amid mounting US pressure

world.headline: Sudan Hospital Strike Kills 10 as Orban Faces Rare Political Threat
world.body:
A drone strike on a hospital in Sudan killed at least 10 people, according to medical charity MSF. The attack is the latest in a pattern of strikes on civilian infrastructure as the country's civil war grinds on with little international intervention.

In Europe, Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban faces what analysts describe as the most serious electoral challenge of his 16-year rule, with opposition forces consolidating around a credible rival. Separately, two dozen US states sued the Trump administration over restrictions on mail-in ballots, and a federal judge upheld the dismissal of subpoenas targeting Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.

sources.title: After 16 years in power, can Viktor Orban finally be unseated?
Two dozen Democrat-led states sue Trump over mail-in ballot limits
US judge upholds decision to toss subpoenas into Fed Chair Jerome Powell
Drone strike on Sudan hospital kills 10, medical charity MSF says

ai.headline: Anthropic Moves Fast: Biotech Deal, PAC, and Market Surge
ai.body:
Anthropic is having a breakout week. The company acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a political action committee to shape AI policy, and is commanding serious attention in private markets - though analysts warn a SpaceX IPO could suck oxygen from the room by competing for the same investor dollars.

Meanwhile, Anthropic tightened its grip on Claude access by forcing OpenClaw users onto a more expensive subscription tier, effectively pricing out a popular third-party client. OpenClaw already had security problems drawing scrutiny, giving Anthropic cover for the move. Users are not happy.

sources.title: Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Executives as AGI Lead Steps Away
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap picks up a new 'special projects' role, while Fidji Simo is taking medical leave shortly after joining. The company's AGI lead is also stepping back, leaving a notable gap at the division tasked with building the technology OpenAI was founded to chase.

The timing is awkward. OpenAI is under commercial pressure from Anthropic and navigating a complicated corporate restructuring. Losing multiple senior figures at once, whatever the reasons, adds uncertainty to an already turbulent moment.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: Musk Demands Banks Buy Grok to Work on SpaceX IPO
ai.body:
Elon Musk is reportedly requiring investment banks pursuing SpaceX IPO business to purchase Grok subscriptions, bundling access to a lucrative deal with a mandate to support his AI product. It is a brazen use of leverage, and banks appear to be complying rather than walking away from one of the most anticipated listings in years.

The move raises obvious conflict-of-interest questions and fits a pattern of Musk using his companies' market power to cross-promote his other ventures.

sources.title: Elon Musk insists banks working on SpaceX IPO must buy Grok subscriptions

ai.headline: AI Is Eroding Critical Thinking, and Data Centers Face Backlash
ai.body:
New research finds that heavy AI users increasingly defer to model outputs without applying their own judgment - a pattern researchers are calling 'cognitive surrender.' The finding lands as AI tools become embedded in daily work and decision-making, raising questions about long-term effects on reasoning skills.

Separately, public appetite for AI infrastructure is souring. Surveys show communities prefer Amazon warehouses to data centers as neighbors, citing noise, water use, and power demands. That resistance is compounding problems for Trump's AI buildout push, which is already being undercut by his own tariffs raising hardware costs.

sources.title: "Cognitive surrender" leads AI users to abandon logical thinking, research finds
Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?
People would rather have an Amazon warehouse in their backyard than a data center

btc.headline: Schwab Enters Spot Crypto Trading as Institutional Appetite Grows
btc.body:
Charles Schwab plans to launch direct spot Bitcoin and Ether trading in the first half of 2026, a move that signals mainstream brokerages are done sitting out the crypto market. The timing matters. Bitcoin ETFs are already drawing comparisons to gold ETFs, with analyst James Seyffart arguing they will eventually surpass gold funds in size - a claim that would have seemed absurd just two years ago.

The Ethereum Foundation is also pushing deeper into its own ecosystem, nearing its 70,000 staked ETH target. Together, these moves paint a picture of institutional and foundational players doubling down on crypto infrastructure rather than retreating.

sources.title: Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst
Ethereum Foundation nearly reaches 70,000 staked ETH goal
Schwab plans spot bitcoin, ether trading launch in first half of 2026

btc.headline: Circle Faces Backlash After $285M Hack Goes Unaddressed
btc.body:
Circle is under pressure after failing to freeze USDC stolen in a $285 million hack of Drift, reigniting a long-running debate about whether stablecoin issuers should act as de facto financial cops. Critics argue Circle's inaction undermined one of the core selling points of regulated stablecoins - the ability to intervene when funds are clearly stolen.

The controversy lands at a sensitive moment. The OCC's approval of a Coinbase trust charter is already drawing opposition from US community banks, who see crypto firms gaining regulatory footholds without equivalent obligations. Both stories point to the same tension: crypto wants institutional legitimacy but resists institutional accountability.

sources.title: Circle under fire after $285 million Drift hack over inaction to freeze stolen USDC
US community banks oppose OCC's approval of Coinbase trust charter

btc.headline: Quantum Threat to Bitcoin Is Real but Distant
btc.body:
Headlines claiming quantum computers can crack Bitcoin in nine minutes are misleading. The research describes a theoretical attack on Bitcoin's elliptic curve cryptography under highly specific conditions - not a practical threat to the network today. Current quantum hardware remains nowhere near the scale required.

Still, the underlying concern is legitimate. Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations will eventually need upgrading, and the window to plan that transition is finite. The story is less about imminent danger and more about whether the Bitcoin development community moves fast enough before the hardware catches up.

sources.title: Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

btc.headline: Cambodia Targets Crypto Scammers With Prison Time
btc.body:
Cambodian lawmakers are proposing serious prison sentences for crypto scammers, a notable shift for a country long associated with Southeast Asia's fraud networks. The legislation signals that regional governments are moving from passive observation to active enforcement as crypto scams continue to drain billions from victims globally.

The proposal also reflects growing geopolitical pressure on crypto crime hubs. Whether Cambodia follows through with enforcement - rather than legislation alone - will determine whether the crackdown has real teeth.

sources.title: Cambodian lawmakers propose severe prison time for crypto scammers

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust tart filled with slow-cooked leeks caramelised in white miso, topped with toasted walnuts and a whisper of thyme.
food.tip: Blind-bake the pastry shell for 12 minutes before adding the filling — leeks release moisture and a soggy base will ruin the whole thing.

exercise.name: The Farmer's Saturday
exercise.equipment: Two kettlebells or heavy dumbbells, a pull-up bar (optional)
exercise.description: A loaded carry and grip-strength circuit — farmer carries, suitcase deadlifts, and dead hangs — that builds functional strength through the hands, shoulders, and core in a way machines never replicate.

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — used by the nervous systems of crayfish, by neurons in the human brain, and increasingly in engineering.
learn.why: It quietly dismantles the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and once you understand it you start seeing its fingerprints in biology, economics, and perception science — a genuinely mind-shifting Saturday rabbit hole.
